text: Laparoscopic repair of left periduodenal hernia, a video presentation from the Mount Sinai Hospital.

Patient is a twenty-six-year-old male with no past medical or surgical history who has been experiencing episodic abdominal pain every five years for the last fifteen years. He had two episodes in the last five months which required emergency room visits.

Upper GI series showed a cluster of jejunum just left of the duodenum in the left upper quadrant, consistent with the left paraduodenal hernia. CT scan showed an encapsulated circumscribed mass of the small bowel in the left upper quadrant, also consistent with a left periduodenal hernia.

Periduodenal hernias can be right or left-sided. Right-sided periduodenal hernias are characterized by small bowel herniating posterior to the superior mesenteric vessels through the fossa of wall dire. Jejunum is typically trapped in the right upper quadrant behind the right mesocolon and mesentery of the cecum. In left peridodinal hernias, bowel passes through the fossa of Lanzerz.

The laparoscopic view shows the small bowel trapped between the left mesocolon and the posterior abdominal wall. The free edge of the left mesocolon is visible, containing the inferior mesenteric vein and the ascending left colic artery.

The surgeon initiates the laparoscopic hernia repair by making an incision in the left subcostal region, employing an optical viewing technique. Six 5mm ports are utilized for access into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon identifies the cecum and terminal ileum in the right lower quadrant and traces the small bowel retrograde, revealing abnormal adhesions between loops of small bowel and the small bowel mesentery.
The surgeon reflects a loop of small bowel towards the right abdomen, revealing an abnormal fusion between the left mesocolon and the small bowel mesentery.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate the left mesocolon from the small bowel mesentery, carefully freeing the adhered structures.

After lysing the adhesions, the surgeon visualizes the fossa of Lanzert and the hernia sac containing loops of small bowel. The surgeon then reduces multiple loops of small bowel trapped within the hernia sac.

The surgeon visualizes the fossa of Lanzert, located left of the fourth portion of the duodenum and posterior to the left mesocolon, which contains the inferior mesenteric vein. After completely reducing the small bowel, the ligament of Treitz is identified by running the small bowel retrograde.

The surgeon begins the hernia repair by closing the hernia orifice in two layers. The first layer involves placing a purse string silk suture to approximate the small bowel mesentery, proximal jejunum, mesocolon, and transverse colon.
The surgeon applies a running silk suture to reinforce the approximation of the proximal jejunum, mesocolon, and transverse colon, completing the second layer of the hernia repair.
The surgeon completes the hernia repair procedure by applying fibrin sealant over the closed hernia orifice, providing additional reinforcement to the surgical site.
On post-op day number one, the patient underwent an upper GI series that showed the small bowel to be in the correct position. He tolerated diet and was discharged home on post-op day number one.
He was seen for follow-up at three weeks with no complaints.

Paraduodenal hernias result from congenital malformations forming potential spaces near ligament of trites. They accounted for thirty to fifty percent of internal hernias before gastric bypasses were common. Most patients present in the fourth to sixth decade of life with obstructive or ischemic bowel symptoms.
Diagnoses can be made on CT scan or upper GI series. In left paraduodenal hernia, CT and upper GI series show loops of jejunum in the left upper quadrant, lateral to the fourth portion of duodenum.

Right paraduodenal hernias on CT demonstrate loops of jejunum in the right upper quadrant behind the mesocolon. Treatment for both types is surgery. Approach can be laparoscopic or open. Important steps of the operation include lysis of the adhesions, reduction of hernia, bowel resection if necessary, and closure of the defect.

In summary, periduodenal hernias are congenital defects near the ligament trites resulting from malrotation. Left periduodenal hernias occur through the fossa of Lanzert. Right periduodenal hernias occur through the fossa of Waldeyer. Diagnosis can be made with CT or upper GI series. Treatment can be performed through laparoscopic surgical reduction of bowel and primary closure of the defect.
